one_line_takeaway: UNIGUARD adds a PGD-optimized image noise plus an optimized-or-pre-defined text suffix as a transferable inference-time guardrail, cutting multimodal jailbreak success on LLaVA from ~82% to ~25% while barely touching A-OKVQA accuracy, though transfer and fluency are uneven and it assumes a non-adaptive attacker.
key_finding: On LLaVA 1.5 under unconstrained visual attack plus RTP text, UNIGUARD cuts attack success ratio from 81.61% (No Defense) to 25.17% (optimized text variant) / 25.69% (pre-defined text variant), beating the best baseline VLGuard at 33.42%. The paper frames this as a ~55% absolute improvement over the raw model and ~12% over the best baseline. Ablation shows the image guardrail contributes more than the text guardrail (image-only at epsilon=64/255 reaches 38.78% vs text-only optimized L=16 at 56.21%), and combining both modalities is best. Utility is largely preserved: A-OKVQA accuracy drops only from 0.820 to 0.818 for UNIGUARD(O) (and 0.772 for the pre-defined variant), far better than DiffPure which collapses to 0.412. The guardrail transfers across models but unevenly: on InstructBLIP the optimized text variant barely helps (59.35% vs 59.80% no-defense) and only the pre-defined variant works (43.79%). The optimized text guardrail improves security but harms fluency (high perplexity) because it appends non-natural-language tokens.
core_contribution: UNIGUARD is an inference-time, multimodal safety guardrail for defending aligned multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) against jailbreak attacks. Its own contribution is a jointly-optimized pair of guardrails: (1) an additive image 'safety noise' v_sg bounded by an L-infinity constraint epsilon, optimized with Projected Gradient Descent to minimize the MLLM's log-likelihood of generating harmful sentences from a small toxic corpus C; and (2) a text guardrail x_sg produced by a gradient-based top-K discrete token search (adapted from AutoPrompt/GCG-style search) that is appended as a suffix, with a simpler human-written pre-defined alternative ('DO NOT include harmful content in your response'). The two guardrails are trained jointly (the image guardrail is present while optimizing text tokens) so the text guardrail captures cross-modal signal. At inference the guardrails are simply added to the image and appended to the text, requiring no backward passes or gradient computation, and the guardrail contents are kept accessible only to developers so an attacker cannot see them. The stated novelty is being a holistic defense spanning multiple modalities, multiple MLLM architectures, and multiple attack types, versus prior unimodal-only or single-model defenses.
threat_model: A conversational MLLM takes user image and/or text input. An adversary crafts multimodal jailbreak inputs to make an aligned MLLM emit toxic/harmful content or attacker-specified phrases. Concretely the paper defends against: (a) the Qi et al. (2023) visual adversarial attack in an unconstrained form (any pixel value in [0,1], treated as the worst case) and a constrained form (epsilon = 64/255 perturbation on COCO images), and (b) adversarial text prompts from RealToxicityPrompts that induce toxic continuations. The defender is the model deployer, who applies the pre-computed image noise and text suffix at inference; the guardrail values are assumed secret from the attacker. The guardrail is optimized once on an open-source base model (LLaVA 1.5) and then transferred, including to black-box proprietary models (GPT-4o, Gemini Pro).
limitations: (1) Guardrails are optimized on and biased toward LLaVA 1.5; transferability is real but uneven, and on InstructBLIP the optimized text guardrail is essentially ineffective (only the pre-defined suffix helps). (2) A security-vs-fluency tradeoff: the optimized text guardrail sharply raises perplexity (e.g., 93.2 for image-only at eps=32/255 vs 5.35 for BlurKernel), and a toxicity-vs-utility tradeoff grows with epsilon (64/255 degrades benign accuracy). (3) Proprietary-model evaluation is weak as a security claim: GPT-4o's own filter already pre-filters ~30% of prompts (only ~10% of the rest jailbreak), and Gemini Pro results required manually disabling its safety filter. (4) Threat model assumes guardrail contents stay secret from the attacker (no adaptive-attacker evaluation against a known guardrail). (5) Only image+text modalities; no audio/video (authors' Appendix D). (6) The corpus C itself is toxic, posing misuse/leakage risk (authors' ethics section). (7) Success is defined solely by Perspective API thresholds, inheriting that classifier's biases.

datasets_used: COCO 2017 validation set (1,000 images with text questions) for benign/adversarial image evaluation
AdvBench (574 harmful strings) used as the harmful corpus C for guardrail optimization
A-OKVQA (1,000 image-question pairs) for benign VQA utility
MM-Vet for vision-language capability sub-scores (Rec, OCR, Know, Gen, Spat, Math)
Zong et al. (2024) Safe-Unsafe and Unsafe subsets for additional attack types
quantitative_results: LLaVA 1.5, unconstrained visual + RTP: attack success No Defense 81.61% -> UNIGUARD(O) 25.17% / UNIGUARD(P) 25.69%; best baseline VLGuard 33.42%
LLaVA 1.5, constrained visual (eps=64/255) + RTP: No Defense 73.73% -> UNIGUARD(O) 19.95% / UNIGUARD(P) 21.52%
Ablation (LLaVA, unconstrained): image-only eps=64/255 = 38.78%, text-only optimized L=16 = 56.21%, combined UNIGUARD(O) = 25.17%
A-OKVQA accuracy: No Defense 0.820, UNIGUARD(O) 0.818, UNIGUARD(P) 0.772, DiffPure 0.412
MiniGPT-4, unconstrained + RTP: No Defense 37.20% -> UNIGUARD(O) 24.98% / UNIGUARD(P) 25.88%
InstructBLIP, unconstrained + RTP: No Defense 59.80% -> UNIGUARD(P) 43.79% (UNIGUARD(O) only 59.35%)
GPT-4o: built-in filter pre-filters ~30% of adversarial prompts, only ~10% of remainder jailbreak; UNIGUARD still improves robustness
Zong et al. attacks on LLaVA-7B: Safe-Unsafe 87.8% -> UNIGUARD 1.8% (VLGuard 2.3%); Unsafe 73.1% -> UNIGUARD 1.3% (VLGuard 1.8%)
Random-noise attack success with UNIGUARD(image+optimized text) = 12.43% vs 25.17% for unconstrained adversarial visual attack
Perplexity: optimized text guardrail raises perplexity (e.g., image-only eps=32/255 at 93.2, UNIGUARD(O) 6.16) vs pre-defined UNIGUARD(P) 4.90 on LLaVA Table 1
baselines_compared: SmoothLLM (Robey et al. 2023, text random-perturbation ensembling)
VLGuard (Zong et al. 2024, multimodal safety fine-tuning)
